human_truth: The system is trying to log in with the wrong username — it's using your Mac's username instead of your server username.
fix_steps: Check what username your server expects (e.g., 'ubuntu' or 'phoenix')
Add 'ansible_user=YOUR_SERVER_USERNAME' to your inventory file next to the IP address
Or use the -u flag when running ansible: ansible-playbook playbook.yml -u ubuntu

human_truth: Your inventory file has the IP address in the wrong place — it needs to come before the username setting, not after.
fix_steps: Open your inventory file
Find the line causing the error
Make sure it follows this format: 192.168.1.1 ansible_user=ubuntu
The IP address always comes first, then the settings after it

human_truth: Your playbook is trying to run as admin (become: yes) but doesn't have the password to do that.
fix_steps: Add the -K flag when running your playbook: ansible-playbook playbook.yml -K
It will then ask you for your sudo password once
Or for a permanent fix: add your user to sudoers with NOPASSWD

robot_error: Colons in unquoted values must be followed by a non-space character
human_truth: There's a hidden extra space after a colon in your YAML file — YAML is very fussy about spaces.
fix_steps: Look at the line and column number in the error (e.g., line 8, column 10)
Go to that exact line in your file
Look for a colon followed by a space where there shouldn't be one
Remove the extra space — or wrap the value in quotes if it needs a colon in it

human_truth: Something in your YAML file is indented at the wrong level — it's like a bullet point that's been pushed too far to the right.
fix_steps: Find the line number mentioned in the error
Check that all your tasks (lines starting with - name:) are at the same indentation level
Use spaces not tabs — YAML does not accept tab characters
Each level of indentation should be exactly 2 spaces

human_truth: You used a word from Docker Compose (environment:) inside an Ansible task — Ansible uses a different word for the same thing.
fix_steps: Find 'environment:' in your Ansible task
Change it to 'env:' instead
These two words mean the same thing but Ansible only accepts 'env:'

human_truth: Your disk might be completely full — when that happens, apps crash with vague errors instead of telling you the real problem.
fix_steps: Run: df -h
Look for any line showing 100% or close to it
If your disk is full: run 'docker system prune -f' to remove unused Docker files
If that doesn't help, you may need to expand your disk or delete old files

human_truth: This is asking if you want to add a password to your SSH key. For automation to work, you must leave this blank.
fix_steps: STOP — do not type a password here
Just press ENTER to leave it blank
Press ENTER again to confirm
If you already added a passphrase, you will need to regenerate your key without one

human_truth: Your VM was given more disk space but Ubuntu hasn't been told to use it yet. The space exists but is locked away.
fix_steps: Run: df -h to check current disk usage
Run: lsblk to see all available disk space
Run: sudo lvextend -l +100%FREE /dev/ubuntu-vg/ubuntu-lv
Then run: sudo resize2fs /dev/ubuntu-vg/ubuntu-lv
Run df -h again to confirm the space is now available

human_truth: The server you are trying to reach is not listening for connections — either the service isn't running, or you have the wrong port number.
fix_steps: Check the service is actually running: sudo systemctl status SERVICE_NAME
Check you have the right port number
Check your firewall isn't blocking the connection: sudo ufw status
Try restarting the service: sudo systemctl restart SERVICE_NAME

human_truth: Your computer can't find the address you gave it — either the hostname doesn't exist, or DNS isn't working.
fix_steps: Check you haven't made a typo in the hostname
Try using the IP address instead of the hostname to test
Check your DNS is working: ping 8.8.8.8
If using a custom hostname, make sure your DNS server is running

human_truth: Git can't find the repository you're pointing at — either the URL is wrong, the repo doesn't exist yet, or you don't have permission to access it.
fix_steps: Check the URL is correct — look for typos in the username or repo name
Make sure the repository actually exists — log into Gitea or GitHub and check
Check you're using the right username — run: git remote -v to see the current URL
If the URL is wrong, fix it with: git remote set-url origin YOUR_CORRECT_URL
If the repo doesn't exist yet, create it first then push

human_truth: Git tried to log in but the username or password was wrong — or your Git config is using a different username than your account.
fix_steps: Check your Git config username: git config --global user.name
Update it if wrong: git config --global user.name 'YOUR_USERNAME'
Update your email too: git config --global user.email 'YOUR_EMAIL'
Try pushing again and enter your correct password when prompted
If it still fails, check the remote URL matches your actual username: git remote -v

robot_error: src refspec main does not match any
human_truth: Git is trying to push a branch called 'main' but it doesn't exist yet — you need to make your first commit before you can push.
fix_steps: Make sure you have at least one commit: git add -A && git commit -m 'first commit'
Then push with: git push origin main
If your branch is called 'master' instead of 'main', use: git push origin master

human_truth: Someone else (or another machine) pushed changes to the repo while you were working. Your copy is behind — you need to grab those changes before you can push yours.
fix_steps: Run: git pull --rebase origin main
This grabs the latest changes and puts your work on top
Then push again: git push origin main
If you're using GitSpeak, the 'save' command handles this automatically

robot_error: fatal: Need to specify how to reconcile divergent branches
human_truth: Your local copy and the remote copy have both changed independently — Git doesn't know which version to trust and needs you to decide.
fix_steps: Run: git pull --rebase origin main
Rebase keeps your history clean and is the recommended option
If you get conflicts after rebasing, open the files marked as conflicted and choose which changes to keep

robot_error: nothing to commit, working tree clean
human_truth: There's nothing new to save — all your files are already committed. This isn't an error, it just means everything is up to date locally.
fix_steps: This is not actually an error — your files are already saved
If you're trying to push to remote, run: git push origin main
If you expected changes to be here, check you saved your files in your editor first
Run: git status to see exactly what state everything is in

robot_error: Recv failure: Connection reset by peer
human_truth: The connection to your Git server was suddenly cut off mid-transfer — usually because the server went down or restarted while you were pushing.
fix_steps: Check your Gitea server is still running: docker ps | grep gitea
Wait 30 seconds and try again
Try pushing again: git push origin main
If it keeps happening, check if OrbStack or Docker has crashed

robot_error: Cannot connect to the Docker daemon
human_truth: Docker isn't running on your machine. It needs to be started before you can use any Docker commands.
fix_steps: On Mac: open OrbStack or Docker Desktop from your Applications folder
On Linux: run sudo systemctl start docker
Wait 10-15 seconds for it to fully start
Then try your Docker command again

robot_error: Bind for 0.0.0.0:80 failed: port is already allocated
human_truth: Something else on your machine is already using that port. Two things can't share the same port at the same time.
fix_steps: Find what's using the port: sudo ss -tlnp | grep :80 (replace 80 with your port)
Either stop the conflicting service, or change your container to use a different port
To change the port in docker-compose, edit the ports section: '8081:80' instead of '80:80'
Then restart your container: docker compose down && docker compose up -d

human_truth: Docker is trying to use an image that hasn't been downloaded yet. It needs to pull it from the internet first.
fix_steps: This usually fixes itself — Docker will automatically download the image
If it fails, check your internet connection
Try pulling it manually: docker pull IMAGE_NAME
If the image name is wrong, check the exact name on hub.docker.com

robot_error: docker: 'compose' is not a docker command
human_truth: You're using an older version of Docker that needs a hyphen — it's 'docker-compose' not 'docker compose'.
fix_steps: Try using 'docker-compose' instead of 'docker compose'
Or update Docker to a newer version that supports the newer syntax
On Mac: update OrbStack or Docker Desktop
On Linux: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade docker-ce

human_truth: pip (Python's package installer) isn't installed or isn't on your PATH. You need it to install Python packages.
fix_steps: Try pip3 instead of pip
On Mac: brew install python3 (includes pip)
On Ubuntu: sudo apt install python3-pip
After installing, close and reopen your terminal

human_truth: Python is trying to use a package that hasn't been installed yet. Like trying to use an app you haven't downloaded.
fix_steps: Install the missing module with pip: pip install MODULE_NAME
Replace MODULE_NAME with whatever name appeared in the error
If pip isn't found, try: pip3 install MODULE_NAME
After installing, try running your script again

human_truth: Python found something in your code it doesn't understand — usually a missing bracket, quote, or colon.
fix_steps: Go to the line number shown in the error
Look for: missing closing bracket ), ] or }
Missing colon at the end of a def, if, for, or while line
A quote that was opened but never closed
Something spelled wrong that Python doesn't recognise as a keyword

human_truth: Python is very strict about spacing — a line has been indented (pushed to the right) when it shouldn't be, or not indented when it should be.
fix_steps: Go to the line number shown in the error
Check that the indentation (spaces at the start) matches the lines around it
Make sure you're using spaces, not tabs — Python prefers spaces
Each level of indentation should be 4 spaces

human_truth: You're trying to do something with a file or folder that your user account isn't allowed to do.
fix_steps: Try adding 'sudo' before your command to run it as admin
If editing a file: sudo nano FILENAME
If running a script: sudo ./script.sh
Be careful with sudo — it has full access to your system

human_truth: The command you typed doesn't exist on your system — either it's not installed, or the name is slightly wrong.
fix_steps: Check for typos in the command name
If it's a tool you installed, try closing and reopening your terminal
If it's a new alias, run: source ~/.zshrc to reload your shell config
If the tool genuinely isn't installed, install it first

human_truth: Another package installation is already running in the background. Two things can't install packages at the same time.
fix_steps: Wait a few minutes and try again — a background update might be running
Find what's holding the lock: sudo lsof /var/lib/apt/lists/lock
If nothing is running, remove the lock file: sudo rm /var/lib/apt/lists/lock
Then try your apt command again

human_truth: Ubuntu can't find the package you're trying to install — either it's in a different repository, the name is wrong, or you need to update your package list first.
fix_steps: First update your package list: sudo apt update
Then try installing again: sudo apt install PACKAGE_NAME
Double-check the exact package name — try searching: apt search PACKAGE_NAME
If it's still not found, you may need to add the software's repository first

robot_error: WARNING: REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED
human_truth: The server you're connecting to has a different identity than what your computer remembers. This usually happens after reinstalling a server — but could occasionally indicate something suspicious.
fix_steps: If you recently reinstalled or rebuilt the server, this is expected and safe to fix
Remove the old key: ssh-keygen -R HOSTNAME_OR_IP
Then connect again — it will ask you to accept the new key
Type 'yes' to accept and add the new key

robot_error: UNREACHABLE! Failed to connect to the host
human_truth: Ansible can't connect to your server at all — the server might be off, the IP address might be wrong, or SSH might not be running.
fix_steps: Check the server is actually running
Try connecting manually first: ssh username@IP_ADDRESS
Make sure the IP address in your inventory.yml is correct
Make sure SSH is installed on the server: sudo apt install openssh-server

human_truth: Ansible needs admin access to run this task, but the server is asking for a password and Ansible doesn't have one to give.
fix_steps: Run your playbook with the -K flag: ansible-playbook playbook.yml -K
It will ask for your sudo password once
For a permanent fix, set up passwordless sudo on the server
Run on the server: echo 'USERNAME ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL' | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/USERNAME

human_truth: You're in a special Git mode where you're looking at old code but not on any branch — changes you make here might get lost.
fix_steps: To get back to normal: git checkout main
Or if your branch is called master: git checkout master
If you made changes you want to keep, create a new branch first: git checkout -b my-new-branch
Then commit your changes to that branch

human_truth: Two different versions of the same file were changed in different places and Git doesn't know which version to keep — it needs you to decide.
fix_steps: Open the file mentioned in the error
Look for lines starting with <<<<<<< HEAD — that's your version
Lines after ======= are the other version
Lines ending with >>>>>>> are the end of the conflict
Delete the markers and keep the version you want (or combine both)
Then: git add FILENAME && git commit

human_truth: A JavaScript package your project needs hasn't been installed yet — like trying to run a recipe without the ingredients.
fix_steps: Run: npm install (in the project folder)
Wait for it to finish downloading all the packages
Then try running your project again
If npm isn't found, install Node.js first from nodejs.org

human_truth: The port your app is trying to use is already taken by another process — two apps can't share the same port.
fix_steps: Find what's using the port: sudo lsof -i :PORT_NUMBER (replace PORT_NUMBER)
Either stop that process, or change your app to use a different port
To kill the process using the port: kill -9 PROCESS_ID
Or simply restart your computer to clear all port bindings

human_truth: The website you're trying to connect to has an SSL certificate problem — either it's expired, self-signed, or your system's certificate list is out of date.
fix_steps: Update your system's certificate list: sudo apt update && sudo apt install ca-certificates
If it's a local self-hosted service with a self-signed certificate, add -k to your curl command (for testing only)
If it's a real website, the site may have an expired certificate — try again later
Never use -k in production — it disables security checks

robot_error: Failed to start service. Unit not found
human_truth: You're trying to start or stop a service that doesn't exist on this system — either it's not installed or the name is spelled differently.
fix_steps: Check the exact service name: systemctl list-units --type=service | grep SERVICE_NAME
Make sure the software is actually installed first
The service name might be slightly different — try tab completion: sudo systemctl start SERVICE and press TAB
Check if the service file exists: ls /etc/systemd/system/

robot_error: Updates were rejected because the tip of your current branch is behind
human_truth: Your local branch is behind the remote — someone or something pushed changes you don't have yet. You need to catch up before pushing.
fix_steps: Pull the latest changes first: git pull --rebase origin main
Then push again: git push origin main
If you're using GitSpeak, the 'save' command handles this automatically

human_truth: You're trying to remove a Docker volume that still has a container using it — you need to stop the container first.
fix_steps: Find which container is using it: docker ps -a
Stop the container: docker stop CONTAINER_NAME
Remove the container: docker rm CONTAINER_NAME
Now you can remove the volume: docker volume rm VOLUME_NAME

robot_error: ERROR! Syntax Error while loading YAML
human_truth: Your YAML file has a formatting mistake — YAML is very strict about how things are laid out and something doesn't look right.
fix_steps: Look at the line number in the error message
Common causes: tabs instead of spaces, missing colon, wrong indentation
Use a YAML validator to check your file — paste it into yamllint.com
Make sure all list items start with '- ' (dash space)
